corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Overall, our DPR-based models outperform the previous stateof-the-art results on four out of the five datasets, with 1% to 12% absolute differences in exact match accuracy.

While both methods include additional pretraining tasks and employ an expensive end-to-end training regime, DPR manages to outperform them on both NQ and TriviaQA, simply by focusing on learning a strong passage retrieval model using pairs of questions and answers.
Although the results of DPR on WQ and TREC in the single-dataset setting are less competitive, adding more question-answer pairs helps boost the performance, achieving the new state of the art.
Below we briefly describe each dataset and refer readers to their paper for the details of data preparation.

CuratedTREC (TREC) sources questions from TREC QA tracks 5 However, In this section, we evaluate the retrieval performance of our Dense Passage Retriever (DPR), along with analysis on how its output differs from Training Retriever Top-20 Top-100 NQ TriviaQA WQ TREC SQuAD NQ TriviaQA WQ TREC SQuAD None BM25 59.1 66.9 55.0 70.9 68.8 73.7 76.7 71.1 84.1 80.0 Single DPR 78.4 79.4 73.2 79.8 63.2 85.4 85.0 81.4 89.1 77.2 BM25 + DPR 76.6 79.8 71.0 85.2 71.5 83.8 84.5 80.5 92.7 81.3 Multi DPR 79.4 78.8 75.0 89.1 51.6 86.0 84.7 82.9 93.9 67.6 BM25 + DPR 78.0 79.9 74.7 88.5 66.2 83.9 84.4 82.3 94.1 78.6 Table 2: Top-20 & Top-100 retrieval accuracy on test sets, measured as the percentage of top 20/100 retrieved passages that contain the answer.
While it is good to have the flexibility to adapt the retriever to each dataset, it would also be desirable to obtain a single retriever that works well across the board.
We used λ = 1.1 based on the retrieval accuracy in the development set.
Table 2 compares different passage retrieval systems on five QA datasets, using the top-k accuracy (k ∈ {20, 100}).

BM25 parameters b = 0.4 (document length normalization) and k1 = 0.9 (term frequency scaling) are tuned using development sets. tiple datasets, TREC, the smallest dataset of the five, benefits greatly from more training examples.
Results can be improved further in some cases by combining DPR with BM25 in both single-and multi-dataset settings.
In other words, can it still generalize well when directly applied to a different dataset without additional fine-tuning?
To test the cross-dataset generalization, we train DPR on Natural Questions only and test it directly on the smaller WebQuestions and CuratedTREC datasets.
We find that DPR generalizes well, with 3-5 points loss from the best performing fine-tuned model in top-20 retrieval accuracy (69.9/86.3 vs.
In this section, we experiment with how different passage retrievers affect the final QA accuracy.
Table summarizes our final end-to-end QA results, measured by exact match with the reference answer after minor normalization as in .

From the table, we can see that higher retriever accuracy typically leads to better final QA results: in all cases except SQuAD, answers extracted from the passages retrieved by DPR are more likely to be correct, compared to those from BM25.
Overall, our DPR-based models outperform the previous stateof-the-art results on four out of the five datasets, with 1% to 12% absolute differences in exact match accuracy.
Although the results of DPR on WQ and TREC in the single-dataset setting are less competitive, adding more question-answer pairs helps boost the performance, achieving the new state of the art.
We also note that we found k = 50 to be optimal for NQ, and k = 10 leads to only marginal loss in exact match accuracy (40.8 vs.

In comparison, DPR is able to return Table 6: Retrieval Top-k accuracy on the development set of Natural Questions using different similarity and loss functions. the correct answer, presumably by matching "body of water" with semantic neighbors such as sea and channel, even though no lexical overlap exists.
59.1% for top-20 accuracy on Natural Questions).

In this work, we show that retrieval can be practically implemented using dense representations alone, where embeddings are learned from a small number of questions and passages by a simple dualencoder framework.

When evaluated on a wide range of open-domain QA datasets, our dense retriever outperforms a strong Lucene-BM25 system greatly by 9%-19% absolute in terms of top-20 passage retrieval accuracy, and helps our end-to-end QA system establish new state-of-the-art on multiple open-domain QA benchmarks.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
